title: Formal Risk Analysis Conducted
description: Has your organization conducted a thorough, documented risk analysis to identify all potential threats and vulnerabilities to ePHI?
guidance: OCR requires a comprehensive, organization-wide analysis. Evidence includes a written report documenting: data flows, threat sources, existing controls, and likelihood/impact of threats. Should be updated whenever significant changes occur and at least annually.
lookFor: A written risk analysis document (not just a mental checklist)
An inventory of all systems that store or transmit ePHI
A list of identified threats for each system
Evidence the analysis was updated in the last 12 months
Clear record of who performed and approved the analysis

title: Risk Management Plan Implemented
description: Has your organization implemented a risk management plan that addresses identified risks to an acceptable level?
guidance: A risk management plan should document specific mitigation strategies for each identified risk, assign responsibilities, set timelines, and track remediation. It is a living document updated as risks change.
lookFor: A written plan with specific mitigation steps for each identified risk
Assigned owners (names or roles) for each remediation task
Target dates and deadlines for remediation
Evidence the plan is reviewed and updated regularly
Status tracking showing progress on open items

title: Workforce Sanction Policy
description: Does your organization have a documented sanction policy that addresses consequences for workforce members who violate security policies?
guidance: Policy must be in writing and communicated to all staff. It should define a range of sanctions proportionate to the severity of the violation, from verbal warning to termination. All workforce members should sign an acknowledgment.
lookFor: A written sanction policy document
A range of consequences (verbal warning through termination)
Evidence all staff have been notified of the policy
Signed acknowledgment forms from employees
Records of any sanctions that have been applied

title: Information System Activity Review
description: Does your organization regularly review information system activity, including audit logs, access reports, and security incident tracking?
guidance: Regular review (at minimum monthly) of audit logs, login reports, and failed access attempts is required. Designate who performs the review, how often, and how anomalies are escalated. Review activity should be documented.
lookFor: A designated person responsible for reviewing system logs
A documented schedule (monthly at minimum)
Written records of each review showing what was checked
A process for escalating suspicious activity
Evidence that reviews are actually happening on schedule

title: Complete ePHI System Inventory
description: Has your organization created and maintained a complete, current inventory of all information systems that create, receive, maintain, or transmit ePHI? This includes EHR, billing software, email, cloud storage, medical devices, patient portals, telehealth platforms, and any mobile apps.
guidance: OCR specifically looks for a documented inventory of all ePHI-touching systems. This is the foundation of the entire SRA — you cannot assess risk for systems you have not identified. The inventory should include: system name, vendor, type of ePHI stored, location (on-prem vs cloud), and data flow connections to other systems.
lookFor: A written inventory document listing all ePHI systems
All EHR, billing, and communication systems listed
Cloud services and SaaS tools included
Medical devices with data connectivity listed
Inventory reviewed and updated within the last 12 months

title: HIPAA Security Officer Designated
description: Has your organization formally designated a Security Officer responsible for the development and implementation of HIPAA security policies?
guidance: The designation must be in writing (job description, policy, or formal letter). The Security Officer must have adequate authority, resources, and training to carry out their responsibilities. Small practices may combine this with the Privacy Officer role.
lookFor: Written designation (letter, job description, or policy document)
The person has received HIPAA security training
The person has authority to make security decisions
Staff know who the Security Officer is

title: HIPAA Privacy Officer Designated
description: Has your organization formally designated a Privacy Officer responsible for HIPAA privacy compliance and patient rights?
guidance: Required under the Privacy Rule (45 CFR §164.530(a)). Designation must be documented. The Privacy Officer should handle patient rights requests, complaints, and staff training on privacy policies.
lookFor: Written designation documenting who the Privacy Officer is
The person handles patient record access requests
The person manages privacy-related complaints
Contact information is available to patients (e.g., in your Notice of Privacy Practices)

title: ePHI Access Authorization Procedures
description: Are written procedures in place for determining which workforce members should have access to ePHI, based on job role and minimum necessary principle?
guidance: Implement role-based access control policies. Document the process for requesting, approving, and granting access. Access should be limited to the minimum necessary for each job function. Maintain an access authorization log.
lookFor: Written access policy defining roles and their ePHI access levels
A formal process for requesting and approving new access
Evidence that access is limited to minimum necessary for each role
An access authorization log showing who approved each user's access

title: Workforce Clearance Verification
description: Does your organization verify that workforce members are appropriate to access ePHI (e.g., background checks, references) before granting access?
guidance: Background screening for staff with ePHI access is a best practice and may be required by state law. Document the screening process. Temporary or contract staff with ePHI access should be similarly vetted.
lookFor: Background check policy for new hires with ePHI access
Records of completed background checks
Screening of temporary and contract workers
A documented process that HR follows consistently

title: Workforce Termination Procedures
description: Does your organization have documented termination procedures to immediately revoke ePHI system access when employment or contracts end?
guidance: Termination checklist should cover: account deactivation, key/badge return, device recovery, password changes for shared accounts (if any), and forwarding of patient communications. Access must be revoked on or before the last day of employment.
lookFor: A written termination checklist that includes IT access revocation
System accounts disabled on or before the last day
Return of keys, badges, laptops, and other devices documented
Shared account passwords changed after departure
Evidence this process is followed consistently

title: Access to ePHI Formally Controlled
description: Does your organization have formal written policies governing who is authorized to access ePHI, under what circumstances, and through which systems?
guidance: Policies should address: authorization levels by role, approval workflow, multi-system access, and restrictions on copying or printing ePHI. Policies should be annually reviewed and acknowledged by all staff.
lookFor: Written access control policies reviewed within the last year
Policies cover all systems with ePHI (EHR, billing, email, etc.)
Rules about printing, copying, or downloading patient data
Staff acknowledgment signatures on the access policies

title: Access Modification on Role Change
description: Are procedures in place to promptly modify or revoke ePHI access rights when a workforce member's role changes (promotion, transfer, demotion)?
guidance: Role changes should trigger an access review within 24-48 hours. Verify that new access is provisioned correctly and old access (no longer appropriate) is removed. HR should notify IT/Security Officer upon any role change.
lookFor: A process linking HR role changes to IT access modifications
Evidence that access is reviewed within 24-48 hours of role changes
Old unnecessary access removed, not just new access added
Documentation of each access change and who approved it

title: Periodic Security Awareness Training
description: Does your organization provide periodic HIPAA security awareness training to all workforce members who handle ePHI?
guidance: Training must be provided at hire and periodically thereafter (OCR recommends annually). Training records (completion dates, attendees, topics) must be retained for 6 years. Training should be updated when threats or policies change.
lookFor: Training provided at onboarding for all new hires
Annual refresher training for all staff
Completion records with dates, names, and topics covered
Training content updated when policies or threats change
Records retained for at least 6 years

title: Malware & Phishing Awareness Training
description: Does workforce training address how to identify and protect against malware, phishing emails, ransomware, and social engineering attacks?
guidance: With 95% of breaches involving human error, phishing simulation and awareness training is critical. Training should include: how to identify suspicious emails, what to do if a link is clicked, how to report incidents, and safe password practices.
lookFor: Phishing-specific training content (not just generic HIPAA training)
Examples of real-world phishing emails and social engineering tactics
Instructions on what to do if a suspicious link is clicked
How to report a suspected phishing attempt

title: Login Monitoring & Alerts
description: Does your organization monitor for and alert on suspicious or unauthorized login activity to ePHI systems?
guidance: Systems should generate alerts for: failed login attempts (brute force), logins outside business hours, logins from unusual locations, or logins to deactivated accounts. Alerts should be reviewed by the Security Officer promptly.
lookFor: Alerts configured for multiple failed login attempts
Monitoring of after-hours logins and unusual access patterns
A designated person who receives and reviews alerts
A process for investigating and documenting alert follow-ups

title: Password Management Procedures
description: Are password management procedures in place and communicated to all staff (complexity requirements, expiration, prohibition on sharing)?
guidance: Password policy should require: minimum 12 characters, mix of character types, no reuse of last 10 passwords, and prohibit sharing. Consider adopting NIST 800-63B guidance (longer passphrases over complex but short passwords). A password manager should be recommended or provided.
lookFor: Written password policy with minimum length and complexity rules
Prohibition on password sharing clearly stated and enforced
Staff trained on creating strong passwords or using a password manager
No sticky notes with passwords visible on workstations

title: Security Incident Response Policy
description: Does your organization have a documented security incident response policy and procedure covering how to identify, report, contain, and remediate security incidents?
guidance: Policy must define: what constitutes a security incident, reporting chain (who to notify and when), containment steps, investigation requirements, documentation, and post-incident review. All staff must know how to report an incident.
lookFor: A written incident response plan/policy
Clear definition of what counts as a security incident
A reporting chain (who to notify first, second, etc.)
Steps for containment, investigation, and remediation
Evidence that all staff know how to report an incident

title: Security Incident Tracking & Documentation
description: Does your organization track, document, and analyze all security incidents, including near-misses and unsuccessful attempts?
guidance: Maintain an incident log for 6 years. Each entry should include: date discovered, nature of incident, systems affected, how it was resolved, and whether ePHI was compromised. Trend analysis should be performed at least annually to identify patterns.
lookFor: An incident log or tracking system (spreadsheet, tool, or app)
Records include: date, description, systems affected, resolution
Near-misses and unsuccessful attempts also documented
Annual trend analysis to identify recurring issues
Records retained for at least 6 years

title: Data Backup Plan — Tested
description: Does your organization have a documented data backup plan, and are backups regularly tested for restoration?
guidance: Backup plan should specify: what data is backed up, backup frequency (daily recommended for ePHI), backup medium (offsite or cloud), encryption of backup media, and a test restoration schedule (at minimum quarterly). A backup that has never been tested is an untested hypothesis.
lookFor: A written backup plan specifying what gets backed up and how often
Daily backups of all ePHI (or more frequent)
Backups stored offsite or in encrypted cloud storage
Quarterly test restorations with documented results
Backup media is encrypted

title: Disaster Recovery Plan
description: Does your organization have a disaster recovery plan (DRP) that documents how to restore ePHI systems after a disaster (fire, flood, hardware failure, ransomware)?
guidance: DRP should define: Recovery Time Objective (RTO — how fast to restore), Recovery Point Objective (RPO — how much data can be lost), steps to restore each critical system, who is responsible, and vendor contacts. Must be tested annually.
lookFor: A written disaster recovery plan
Defined Recovery Time Objective (how fast you need to be back up)
Step-by-step restoration procedures for each critical system
Emergency vendor contact information included
Plan tested at least annually

title: Emergency Mode Operations Plan
description: Does your organization have a plan for maintaining access to critical ePHI during a system outage or emergency?
guidance: The emergency mode plan should address: which ePHI must remain accessible, how to access records if electronic systems are down (paper downtime procedures), who has override access, and how to return to normal operations. Should be practiced during drills.
lookFor: Written downtime procedures for when electronic systems are unavailable
Paper-based forms or backup access methods documented
Staff trained on how to operate during system outages
Defined process for returning to normal operations after the outage

title: Contingency Plan Testing & Revision
description: Are contingency plans (backup, disaster recovery, emergency operations) regularly tested and revised based on test findings?
guidance: Plans that are never tested are unreliable. Annual tabletop exercises or full recovery drills are required. Document the test, findings, and any plan updates made as a result. Test results must be retained for 6 years.
lookFor: Annual test or drill of contingency plans documented
Tabletop exercises or simulated disaster recovery exercises
Written results showing what worked and what didn't
Plan revisions made based on test findings

title: Specific Emergency Types Identified and Planned For
description: Has your organization identified and documented specific types of emergencies that could damage information systems or prevent access to ePHI, and created response plans for each? Types should include: natural disasters (fire, flood, earthquake, hurricane), infrastructure failures (power outage, internet outage, hardware failure), cyberattacks (ransomware, data breach, DDoS), and operational disruptions (pandemic, key personnel loss).
guidance: OCR expects you to have considered multiple emergency scenarios, not just a generic 'emergency plan.' Each identified emergency type should have a documented response procedure. Practices should consider which types are most likely for their geographic location and infrastructure.
lookFor: A written list of emergency types considered
Response procedures for each type
Geographic and environmental risks considered
Cyber incident response separately addressed
Plans reviewed and updated at least annually

title: Security Documentation Retention (6-Year Minimum)
description: Does your organization retain all HIPAA security management documentation — including policies, risk assessments, training records, incident logs, and BAAs — for a minimum of 6 years from the date of creation or last effective date?
guidance: 45 CFR §164.530(j) requires 6-year retention. This applies to policies, SRA reports, training records, incident logs, BAAs, and any action/activity/assessment/designation required by the Security Rule. Electronic retention is acceptable. Ensure backup copies exist.
lookFor: A written retention schedule specifying 6-year minimum
Electronic backup of all compliance documents
Document destruction policy that prevents premature deletion
Retention policy covers all document types (policies, training, incidents, BAAs, SRAs)

title: Business Associates Identified
description: Has your organization identified all Business Associates (vendors, contractors, service providers) that create, receive, maintain, or transmit ePHI on your behalf?
guidance: Maintain a complete, current BA inventory including: name, service provided, type of ePHI accessed, BAA status, and contract renewal date. Common BAs include: EHR vendors, billing companies, transcription services, IT support, cloud storage, medical equipment with data connectivity.
lookFor: A written inventory of all Business Associates
Each entry includes: vendor name, service, and type of ePHI accessed
Common BAs considered: EHR, billing, IT support, cloud services, shredding
Inventory reviewed and updated at least annually

title: BAAs in Place with All Business Associates
description: Are valid, current Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) in place with every identified Business Associate before they access ePHI?
guidance: A BAA is legally required before any BA can access ePHI. BAAs must contain specific required elements per 45 CFR §164.504(e). Verbal agreements are insufficient. Electronic BAAs (e-signatures) are acceptable. Retain all BAAs for 6 years after the relationship ends.
lookFor: Signed BAAs on file for every vendor in your BA inventory
BAAs signed before the vendor accesses any ePHI
BAAs include all HIPAA-required elements
BAAs retained for 6 years after the relationship ends

title: BAA Review & Updates
description: Are BAAs reviewed, updated, and re-executed when business relationships change, contracts are renewed, or regulations are updated?
guidance: BAAs must be updated when: the scope of services changes, the BA subcontracts work, or regulatory changes affect required BAA terms. Set calendar reminders for contract renewals. An outdated or incomplete BAA is treated the same as no BAA by OCR.
lookFor: Calendar reminders set for BAA renewal dates
BAAs updated when scope of services changes
BAAs reviewed when vendors subcontract to other companies
Evidence of regular (at least annual) BAA review

title: Ongoing BA Security Monitoring (Beyond BAAs)
description: Beyond having signed BAAs, does your organization actively monitor and verify that business associates are maintaining adequate security practices? Do BAs communicate security changes, incidents, or personnel changes to your practice? Do you require BAs to provide evidence of their own security measures (e.g., SOC 2 reports, security attestations)?
guidance: A signed BAA alone does not ensure a BA is secure. OCR increasingly expects covered entities to go beyond the paper agreement. Best practices include: requesting annual security attestations from high-risk BAs, requiring BA incident notification within 24 hours, reviewing BA SOC 2 or HITRUST certifications, and conducting periodic BA risk assessments.
lookFor: Process for requesting or reviewing BA security attestations
BA incident notification requirements documented
High-risk BAs provide SOC 2 or equivalent evidence
Annual BA security review conducted
BA communication protocol for security changes documented

title: Technology Asset Inventory (2026 Rule)
description: Has your practice completed a technology asset inventory documenting all systems that create, receive, store, or transmit ePHI?
guidance: The proposed Security Rule update would require a documented inventory of all technology assets handling ePHI, including hardware, software, and cloud services. This goes beyond the general ePHI system inventory — it must cover individual devices, applications, network components, and cloud tenants with sufficient detail to support vulnerability management and incident response.
lookFor: Written inventory of all hardware assets (workstations, servers, mobile devices, network equipment)
Software and SaaS inventory including versions
Cloud services and tenants documented
Each asset tagged with ePHI classification (stores, transmits, or processes)
Inventory reviewed and updated within the last 12 months

title: Network Map & ePHI Data Flow (2026 Rule)
description: Does your practice maintain a current network map showing how ePHI flows between systems?
guidance: Required network topology documentation showing data flows between all systems handling ePHI. The map must show how ePHI enters, moves through, and exits your environment — including connections to business associates, cloud services, and remote access points.
lookFor: A visual network diagram showing all ePHI system connections
Data flow arrows showing how ePHI moves between systems
External connections documented (BA feeds, cloud services, remote access)
Diagram updated within the last 12 months
Diagram reviewed after any infrastructure changes

title: BA Technical Safeguard Verification (2026 Rule)
description: Have you obtained written verification from all business associates confirming their implementation of required technical safeguards?
guidance: Annual written verification from each BA that they have implemented encryption, MFA, audit logging, and incident response capabilities. This goes beyond simply having a signed BAA — you must obtain affirmative proof that BAs are actually implementing the security controls they agreed to.
lookFor: Written verification letters or attestations from each BA
Verification covers encryption, MFA, audit logging, and incident response
Verifications dated within the last 12 months
Process documented for following up with non-responsive BAs
Evidence of action taken when a BA cannot verify compliance

title: 24-Hour BA Incident Notification (2026 Rule)
description: Do your business associate agreements include a 24-hour incident notification requirement?
guidance: The 2026 rule tightens BA incident notification from 60 days to 24 hours. Review all current BAAs to ensure they include this accelerated timeline. BAs must notify covered entities within 24 hours of discovering a security incident involving ePHI, not just confirmed breaches.
lookFor: BAA templates updated with 24-hour notification requirement
Existing BAAs reviewed and amended where needed
Clear definition of 'security incident' in BAA (not just 'breach')
BA contact information current for incident notifications
Internal process documented for receiving and acting on BA notifications

title: Corrective Action Implementation Evidence (2026 Rule)
description: For all corrective actions from your last risk assessment, do you have documented implementation evidence?
guidance: The 2026 rule requires not just a remediation plan, but documented proof that corrective actions were actually implemented. For each corrective action, you should have: what was done, when it was completed, who verified it, and evidence (screenshots, configuration exports, policy documents, training records) confirming the fix is in place.
lookFor: Corrective action tracker with completion status for each item
Evidence artifacts attached to each completed action (screenshots, exports, policy docs)
Verification steps documented (who confirmed the fix works)
Timeline showing when each action was completed vs. deadline
Open items have documented justification and revised timelines

title: Facility Security Plan
description: Does your organization have a documented facility security plan that controls and validates physical access to areas containing ePHI systems?
guidance: The plan should address: which areas require restricted access, what physical controls are used (locks, badges, keypads), how access is granted and revoked, and how the plan is reviewed. Reception areas should not provide a view of computer screens showing patient data.
lookFor: A written facility security plan identifying restricted areas
Server rooms, records rooms, and billing areas identified as restricted
Physical controls (locks, badge readers, keypads) on restricted areas
Reception areas checked — can patients see computer screens?

title: Physical Access Controls to ePHI Areas
description: Are physical access controls (key cards, door locks, access badges, security cameras) in place to limit access to areas housing ePHI systems?
guidance: Evaluate whether server rooms, medical records areas, billing rooms, and other ePHI-heavy areas are locked and access-restricted. Key cards or electronic locks are preferred (they log access). Physical keys should be inventoried and controlled.
lookFor: Server room is locked and access-controlled
Medical records area is secured
Billing room has restricted access
Physical key inventory maintained (who has which keys)
Electronic access logs reviewed periodically

title: Visitor Access Tracking & Supervision
description: Does your organization log and supervise all visitors to areas containing ePHI systems, including maintenance personnel and vendors?
guidance: Visitors should sign in/out with time, purpose, and who they are visiting. They should be escorted in restricted areas. Unescorted vendor/maintenance access to server rooms is a common OCR finding. Badge all visitors with a distinct 'visitor' credential.
lookFor: A visitor sign-in/sign-out log at the entrance
Visitors escorted in restricted areas (server rooms, records areas)
Visitor badges that visually distinguish them from staff
IT vendors and maintenance workers also logged and supervised

title: Maintenance & Repair Records
description: Are maintenance and repair activities on physical components of ePHI systems documented (who performed work, what was done, when)?
guidance: Maintain records for physical infrastructure repairs: HVAC in server areas, electrical work, hardware servicing. Verify maintenance personnel are vetted or supervised. Equipment returned after service should be verified for data integrity. Retain records for 6 years.
lookFor: Maintenance logs for server room and network equipment
Records include: who, what, when, and any equipment removed
Maintenance personnel vetted or escorted during work
Equipment returned from service verified for data integrity

title: Facility Change & Modification Records
description: Does your organization maintain records of all physical changes, upgrades, and modifications to your facility that could affect the security of ePHI? This includes: office renovations, new construction, changes to lock/badge systems, security camera additions or removals, server room modifications, and network infrastructure changes.
guidance: Physical facility changes can create new vulnerabilities or eliminate existing controls. Moving a server, adding a window to a room with ePHI access, or changing lock systems all affect your physical security posture. Maintaining modification records allows you to reassess physical security after changes and demonstrates due diligence to OCR.
lookFor: Log of facility modifications with dates and descriptions
Security impact assessment for significant changes
Server room and restricted area changes documented
Lock/badge system changes recorded
Records retained for at least 6 years

title: Third-Party Physical Access Validation by Role
description: Does your organization validate physical access for third-party visitors (IT vendors, repair technicians, consultants, auditors) based on their specific role and the areas they need to access? Is access limited to only the areas necessary for their work?
guidance: Different third parties need access to different areas. An IT vendor may need server room access but not records storage. A cleaning crew should not have unsupervised access to areas with ePHI workstations. Role-based physical access ensures the principle of minimum necessary applies to facility access, not just data access.
lookFor: Written procedures for validating third-party access requests
Access scoped to specific areas based on work being performed
Escort requirements for sensitive areas
Temporary badge/access credentials that expire
Log of all third-party physical access

title: Workstation Use Policy
description: Are there written policies specifying the appropriate use of workstations that access ePHI, including restrictions on personal use and unauthorized software?
guidance: Policies should address: authorized uses only, prohibition on personal email or social media on clinical workstations, prohibition on installing unauthorized software, clean desk requirements, and what to do before leaving a workstation unattended.
lookFor: Written workstation use policy distributed to all staff
Restrictions on personal use of clinical workstations
Prohibition on installing unauthorized software
Clean desk policy (no patient info left on screens or desktops)
Instructions for locking workstations when stepping away

title: Screen Privacy & Workstation Positioning
description: Are computer screens positioned or protected with privacy screens to prevent unauthorized viewing of ePHI by patients, visitors, or unauthorized staff?
guidance: Walk through your facility as a patient would. Can you see clinical workstation screens from the waiting room, hallway, or reception desk? Privacy screens (polarizing filters) should be applied to any workstation visible to patients. Screen orientation should face away from public areas.
lookFor: Privacy screen filters on monitors visible from public areas
Screens positioned to face away from patient-accessible areas
No ePHI visible from the waiting room, hallways, or check-in desk
Check-in kiosks or tablets have privacy measures

title: Workstation Physical Security
description: Are physical safeguards (cable locks, locked carts, secured mounting) applied to workstations and laptops with ePHI access to prevent theft?
guidance: Desktops should be in locked rooms or secured with cable locks. Laptops should be locked when not in use. Device theft is a leading cause of HIPAA breaches. Portable devices should be inventoried and their location tracked. High-risk areas (labs, exam rooms) need particular attention.
lookFor: Laptops cable-locked or stored in locked cabinets when not in use
Desktops in exam rooms secured (cable lock or locked mount)
An inventory of all devices that access ePHI
Exam room and lab devices checked for physical security

title: Server & Network Equipment Security
description: Are servers, network switches, wireless access points, and other network equipment housed in locked, physically secure areas with limited access?
guidance: Network equipment should be in a dedicated, locked room or cabinet — never in an open area, under a desk, or in a shared closet accessible to patients. If using a closet, it must be locked and access-controlled. Cloud-hosted servers should have contractual physical security protections in BAA.
lookFor: Server in a dedicated, locked room or cabinet
Network switches not in open or patient-accessible areas
Wireless access points physically secured (not easily removable)
Cloud-hosted? Physical security addressed in the vendor's BAA

title: Secure Media Disposal Procedures
description: Does your organization have documented, enforced procedures for securely disposing of hardware and media (hard drives, USB drives, CDs, fax machines, printers) that contain ePHI?
guidance: Simply deleting files or formatting a drive is insufficient — data can be recovered. Procedures must include: NIST 800-88 compliant wiping OR physical destruction (shredding for media, degaussing for magnetic drives). Use a certified media destruction vendor and obtain a certificate of destruction. This includes photocopier hard drives.
lookFor: Written media disposal procedures
Hard drives wiped using certified tools or physically destroyed
Certificates of destruction obtained from disposal vendors
Printers and copiers with hard drives included in disposal procedures
USB drives and CDs also covered by the disposal process

title: Secure Media Re-use (Overwriting)
description: Are secure wiping/overwriting procedures in place before reusing storage media (hard drives, USB drives) that previously contained ePHI?
guidance: Before reassigning any storage device to a new user or purpose, the device must be wiped using a certified multi-pass overwrite tool (e.g., NIST 800-88 Clear) or physically destroyed. Document all wipe events with: device ID, date, method used, and who performed the wipe.
lookFor: Documented procedure for wiping devices before reassignment
Certified wiping tool used (not just format/delete)
Records of each wipe: device ID, date, method, person
Process applied to USB drives and portable media too

title: Hardware & Media Inventory
description: Does your organization maintain a current inventory of all hardware and electronic media that store, process, or transmit ePHI?
guidance: Inventory should include: all computers, laptops, tablets, mobile devices, servers, external drives, USB devices, printers/fax machines, and medical devices with connectivity. Record: device type, serial number, assigned user, location, data stored, and encryption status. Review annually.
lookFor: Written inventory of all devices that store/access ePHI
Each entry includes: device type, serial number, user, location
Includes medical devices with data connectivity
Includes printers, fax machines, and copiers with hard drives
Inventory reviewed and updated at least annually

title: Portable Device Encryption & Tracking
description: Are portable devices (laptops, tablets, smartphones, USB drives) that store or access ePHI encrypted and inventoried?
guidance: Full-disk encryption is required on all portable devices with ePHI. Unencrypted device theft is the most common HIPAA breach type. Use BitLocker (Windows), FileVault (Mac), or equivalent. Mobile Device Management (MDM) should be used to enforce encryption, remote-wipe capability, and PIN locks on mobile devices.
lookFor: Full-disk encryption enabled on all laptops (BitLocker or FileVault)
Smartphones and tablets encrypted with MDM enforced
USB drives encrypted or prohibited
Remote wipe capability for lost/stolen devices
Each portable device tracked in the hardware inventory

title: Data Backup Before Device Movement
description: Is ePHI backed up before workstations or servers are moved, repaired, or sent offsite for service?
guidance: Before any device leaves the facility for repair or replacement, verify that all ePHI has been backed up and that the backup is verified. This prevents data loss during repair. For cloud-hosted data, confirm the cloud provider's backup scope covers all relevant data.
lookFor: Procedure to back up data before any device leaves the facility
Backups verified before the device is released
If cloud-hosted, vendor backup scope confirmed in writing
Process documented and followed consistently

title: Unique User Identification (No Shared Logins)
description: Does every workforce member who accesses ePHI systems have a unique user ID? Are shared or generic logins prohibited?
guidance: Shared logins ('front-desk', 'nurse', 'admin') make audit trails useless and violate HIPAA. Every user must have a unique ID tied to their identity. This is required for any meaningful audit log review. Review all system accounts and disable or convert shared accounts.
lookFor: Every staff member has a unique login for all ePHI systems
No generic accounts like 'frontdesk', 'nurse', 'admin' in use
Shared accounts identified and converted to individual accounts
Audit trail can trace every action to a specific person

title: Emergency Access Procedures
description: Are documented and tested emergency access procedures in place for obtaining ePHI during a system failure, natural disaster, or security incident?
guidance: Emergency access procedures should define: who has break-glass access, how it is activated, what ePHI can be accessed, how emergency access is logged and reviewed post-event. Emergency access accounts should have elevated privileges revoked after the emergency. Test this procedure at least annually.
lookFor: Documented emergency access procedure
Defined who has 'break glass' access authority
Process for logging and reviewing emergency access after the event
Elevated privileges revoked after the emergency ends
Procedure tested at least annually

title: Automatic Session Logoff / Screen Lock
description: Do all systems containing ePHI automatically log off or lock the screen after a defined period of user inactivity?
guidance: Automatic logoff (or screen lock requiring re-authentication) must be enabled on all ePHI systems. Recommended timeout: 5-15 minutes for clinical workstations, up to 30 minutes for back-office. This prevents unauthorized access when staff step away. Configure via Group Policy or EHR settings.
lookFor: Auto-lock or auto-logoff enabled on all clinical workstations
Timeout set to 15 minutes or less for clinical areas
EHR application also has its own session timeout configured
Staff trained to manually lock (Ctrl+L or Win+L) when stepping away

title: Automatic Logoff Timeout Values Configured
description: What specific timeout values are configured for automatic session logoff or screen lock across all ePHI systems? Are these values appropriate for each system's risk level (e.g., clinical workstations in patient areas vs. back-office systems)?
guidance: This goes beyond 'do you have auto-logoff' to verify specific timeout values. Recommended: 5–10 minutes for clinical workstations in patient areas, 15 minutes for general clinical areas, up to 30 minutes for secured back-office areas. EHR application timeout should be configured separately from OS screen lock.
lookFor: Documented timeout values for each ePHI system
OS screen lock configured (5–15 minutes)
EHR application timeout configured separately
Shorter timeouts in patient-accessible areas
Timeout values verified, not just assumed

title: Third-Party Vendor Access Termination Procedures
description: When a contract, BAA, or other arrangement with a third-party vendor ends or changes, do you have documented procedures to immediately terminate or modify their access to your ePHI systems? Does this include remote access, VPN credentials, cloud service accounts, and physical access?
guidance: Workforce termination procedures cover employees, but third-party vendor access termination is a separate concern. Vendors may have remote access (VPN, TeamViewer), cloud admin credentials, or physical keys/badges that must be revoked when the relationship ends. A gap here means former vendors retain ePHI access after contracts end.
lookFor: Written vendor access termination checklist
VPN and remote access credentials revoked on contract end
Cloud service admin accounts removed or transferred
Physical access (keys/badges) collected
Vendor access termination completed within 24 hours of relationship end

title: ePHI Encryption at Rest
description: Is ePHI encrypted at rest on all servers, workstations, databases, and portable storage devices?
guidance: Encryption at rest renders ePHI unreadable if storage media is stolen or improperly disposed. Encryption is the primary 'safe harbor' under the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule — an encrypted device breach may not require patient notification. Verify: full-disk encryption on all endpoints, database encryption, and encrypted cloud storage.
lookFor: Full-disk encryption enabled on all computers and laptops
Database encryption enabled on servers storing ePHI
Cloud storage encryption verified with the vendor
Encryption keys managed securely (not stored with the encrypted data)

title: Audit Logging Enabled on All ePHI Systems
description: Do all systems (EHR, billing software, email, cloud storage) that process or store ePHI have audit logging enabled, capturing who accessed what ePHI and when?
guidance: Audit logs must be enabled for all ePHI systems. At minimum, logs should capture: user ID, timestamp, action performed (view/create/modify/delete), and records accessed. Logs should be stored securely, retained for at least 6 years, and protected from modification.
lookFor: Audit logging enabled on EHR, billing, email, and cloud systems
Logs capture: who, what, when, and what action was taken
Logs retained for at least 6 years
Logs stored securely and protected from tampering

title: Audit Log Review Process
description: Are audit logs reviewed regularly (at minimum monthly) by the Security Officer or designated staff to detect unauthorized access or suspicious patterns?
guidance: Assign responsibility for audit log review in writing. Document the review process, frequency, what is looked for (after-hours access, bulk downloads, access to VIP patients, access by terminated employees), and how anomalies are escalated. Log reviews that are never performed are a consistent OCR finding.
lookFor: Designated person assigned to review logs (in writing)
Reviews happen at least monthly with documented results
Specific checks performed: after-hours access, VIP records, bulk exports
A process for escalating and investigating suspicious findings

title: Audit Log Integrity Protection
description: Are audit logs protected from unauthorized modification, deletion, or tampering? Are logs stored separately from the systems they monitor?
guidance: An attacker or insider threat will attempt to delete logs covering their tracks. Audit logs should be: write-once (append-only), stored on a separate server or SIEM, accessible only to the Security Officer, and backed up offsite. Log integrity verification (hash-based) is a best practice.
lookFor: Logs stored on a separate server or system from what they monitor
Logs are write-once or append-only (can't be edited or deleted)
Access to log files restricted to the Security Officer only
Logs backed up offsite or to a secure cloud service

title: Specific Audit Events Monitored
description: What specific events are monitored in your audit logs? Beyond just enabling logging, does your organization actively track high-risk events including: after-hours ePHI access, access to VIP/employee records, bulk record exports or downloads, failed login attempts, account lockouts, privilege escalation, and new user account creation?
guidance: Enabling audit logging and reviewing logs are covered by other questions. This question focuses on WHAT events are specifically being monitored. OCR investigations frequently reveal that logging was enabled but no one was looking for the right signals. Define specific alert triggers for high-risk events.
lookFor: Written list of monitored events and alert triggers
After-hours access monitoring configured
VIP/employee record access flagged
Bulk export or download alerts in place
Failed login threshold alerts active
New account creation generates notifications

title: ePHI Integrity Verification Mechanisms
description: Are mechanisms in place to ensure that ePHI has not been altered or destroyed without authorization (e.g., checksums, hash verification, version control)?
guidance: ePHI integrity controls prevent unauthorized modifications. Examples: database referential integrity constraints, file hash verification for transmitted documents, version history in EHR, and write-protected audit logs. Evaluate whether your EHR provides data integrity checking and whether it is enabled.
lookFor: EHR version history / change tracking enabled
Database integrity checks or constraints in place
Records show who modified data and when
Protection against unauthorized bulk deletion of records

title: Backup Restoration Testing
description: Are data backups periodically restored in a test environment to verify they are complete, uncorrupted, and usable?
guidance: A backup that cannot be restored is worthless. Perform quarterly test restores of a representative subset of ePHI. Document: what was restored, restore time, whether data was intact, and any issues found. Ransomware incidents often reveal that backups were not functional or were also encrypted.
lookFor: Quarterly test restores performed and documented
Test results show data was complete and usable
Restore time recorded (how long to get back up)
Any issues found during testing are addressed

title: Strong User Authentication
description: Are users required to authenticate with strong credentials (username + password meeting policy requirements) before accessing any ePHI system?
guidance: Verify that all ePHI systems require authentication — there should be no 'auto-login' workstations or systems accessible without credentials. Passwords should meet the organization's password policy. Review all local administrator accounts and disable or rename default accounts.
lookFor: All ePHI systems require username + password authentication
No auto-login or saved-password workstations
Default administrator passwords changed on all systems
No generic or shared passwords in use

title: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for Remote Access
description: Is multi-factor authentication (MFA) required for all remote access, VPN connections, and cloud-based ePHI systems?
guidance: MFA is the single most effective control against credential theft, phishing, and account takeover — the leading causes of healthcare breaches. MFA should be required for: VPN, remote desktop, cloud EHR, Office 365/Google Workspace, and any web portal with ePHI access. Hardware tokens or authenticator apps are preferred over SMS.
lookFor: MFA enabled on VPN and all remote access tools
MFA required for cloud-based EHR, email, and file storage
Authenticator app or hardware token used (SMS is less secure)
MFA enforced by the system, not just recommended in policy
Patient portal admin access also requires MFA

title: Password Policy Technically Enforced
description: Are password complexity, length, expiration, and history requirements technically enforced by systems (not just documented in policy)?
guidance: Policy without technical enforcement is insufficient. Verify that: Active Directory or cloud identity provider enforces minimum password length (12+ chars), complexity, no reuse of last 10 passwords, and account lockout after failed attempts. A documented policy that systems do not enforce will be cited as a gap by OCR.
lookFor: Systems enforce minimum 12-character passwords
Password reuse prevention enabled (last 10 passwords blocked)
Account lockout after 5-10 failed login attempts
Technical enforcement matches what the written policy requires

title: ePHI Encryption in Transit
description: Is all ePHI encrypted when transmitted over networks (internal or external), including between offices, to cloud services, or to Business Associates?
guidance: Use TLS 1.2 or higher for all network transmissions of ePHI. Verify: EHR traffic is HTTPS, inter-office VPN is encrypted, cloud storage connections are encrypted, and interfaces/HL7 feeds use secure protocols. Tools like SSL Labs (ssllabs.com) can verify HTTPS configuration.
lookFor: All web applications use HTTPS (not HTTP)
VPN used for inter-office connections
Cloud service connections encrypted (TLS 1.2 or higher)
HL7 interfaces and data feeds use secure protocols

title: Secure Protocols for Web-Based ePHI
description: Are secure protocols (HTTPS/TLS 1.2+) used for all web-based ePHI access including patient portals, cloud EHR, and staff web applications?
guidance: Verify all web applications handling ePHI use HTTPS with a valid certificate. Redirect HTTP to HTTPS. Deprecated protocols (SSL 3.0, TLS 1.0/1.1) must be disabled. Patient portals and telehealth platforms must also use current TLS. Review your cloud vendors' security documentation.
lookFor: All web portals show HTTPS with a valid certificate
HTTP automatically redirects to HTTPS
Old protocols (SSL 3.0, TLS 1.0/1.1) disabled
Patient portal and telehealth platforms verified for current TLS

title: Secure Email Controls for ePHI
description: Does your organization have controls to prevent unencrypted ePHI from being transmitted via standard email, and are staff trained on secure messaging options?
guidance: Standard email (even over HTTPS) is not secure end-to-end. Controls include: encrypted email (Proofpoint, Virtru, Paubox, or S/MIME), secure patient messaging portals, or policy prohibiting ePHI in email bodies. At minimum, staff should be trained to use a secure channel for any ePHI transmission.
lookFor: Encrypted email solution in place (Virtru, Paubox, Proofpoint, etc.)
Staff trained on when to use secure messaging vs. regular email
Policy prohibiting ePHI in standard email bodies
Secure patient messaging portal available as an alternative

title: Network Firewall & Segmentation
description: Is the practice's network protected by a properly configured firewall, and is the clinical network segmented from guest/public Wi-Fi access?
guidance: A next-generation firewall (NGFW) with IPS should protect the perimeter. Guest Wi-Fi must be on a separate network segment isolated from clinical systems. Medical devices (imaging, lab equipment) should also be segmented. Review firewall rules annually and remove any unnecessary inbound access.
lookFor: Firewall in place protecting the network perimeter
Guest Wi-Fi on a separate network segment from clinical systems
Medical devices on their own network segment if possible
Firewall rules reviewed annually, unnecessary rules removed

title: Anti-Malware Protection
description: Is anti-malware (antivirus, EDR) software installed, centrally managed, and automatically updated on all workstations and servers with ePHI access?
guidance: Traditional antivirus alone is insufficient against modern threats. Consider EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response) solutions. Verify: all endpoints have protection, definitions update automatically, real-time scanning is enabled, and alerts are monitored. Medical devices may require vendor-specific approved solutions.
lookFor: Antivirus/EDR installed on all workstations and servers
Automatic definition updates enabled
Real-time scanning turned on (not just periodic scans)
Alerts monitored by IT or a managed security service
Medical devices checked for compatible antimalware solutions

title: Security Patch Management
description: Is there a documented patch management process to apply security updates to operating systems, applications, and firmware on a timely basis?
guidance: Critical patches should be applied within 30 days of release (72 hours for actively exploited vulnerabilities). Document the patch policy, who is responsible, the patching schedule, and how to handle systems that cannot be patched (compensating controls). Unpatched systems are involved in the majority of ransomware incidents.
lookFor: Written patch management policy with defined timelines
Critical patches applied within 30 days (72 hours if actively exploited)
Someone assigned to monitor for and deploy patches
Systems that can't be patched have compensating controls documented
Patching status tracked and reported regularly

title: Vulnerability Assessments
description: Does your organization conduct periodic vulnerability scans or penetration tests to identify security weaknesses in systems that handle ePHI?
guidance: OCR expects organizations to regularly test the effectiveness of their security controls. Quarterly vulnerability scanning and annual penetration testing are best practices. Findings must be remediated and documented. Even small practices can use affordable tools (Qualys, Tenable, Nessus) or engage an IT security vendor.
lookFor: Quarterly vulnerability scans performed (or more frequently)
Annual penetration testing by a qualified vendor
Findings documented and remediated on a timeline
Scan results retained as compliance evidence

title: Secure Remote Access Controls
description: Are all remote access methods (VPN, RDP, remote desktop software) secured with encryption, MFA, and access logging? Are unnecessary remote access ports disabled?
guidance: Exposed RDP (port 3389) is the #1 ransomware entry point. Remote access should use a VPN + MFA combination, not direct RDP exposure to the internet. Review firewall rules to confirm RDP and other remote access ports are not open to the public internet. Disable TeamViewer, AnyDesk, or similar tools when not actively needed.
lookFor: Remote access uses VPN with MFA (not direct RDP to the internet)
RDP port (3389) not exposed to the public internet
Remote desktop tools (TeamViewer, AnyDesk) disabled when not in use
Remote access sessions logged and auditable
Firewall rules reviewed for unnecessary inbound remote access

title: Emerging Threat Awareness & Response
description: Does your organization stay informed about emerging cybersecurity threats and vulnerabilities that could affect your ePHI systems? Do you subscribe to threat intelligence sources (HHS HC3 alerts, CISA advisories, vendor security bulletins) and have a process to evaluate and respond to new threats?
guidance: Healthcare is the most-targeted industry for cyberattacks. OCR expects organizations to demonstrate awareness of current threats, not just historical ones. The HHS Health Sector Cybersecurity Coordination Center (HC3) publishes regular threat alerts specific to healthcare. CISA publishes Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) that require urgent response.
lookFor: Subscription to HHS HC3 or CISA alerts
Designated person who monitors threat intelligence
Process for evaluating whether new threats affect your systems
Documented response to at least 2 threat alerts in the past year
Evidence of security posture changes based on threat intelligence

title: Security Configuration Baseline & Review
description: Does your organization maintain documented security configuration baselines for ePHI systems and periodically review configurations to verify they haven't drifted from the baseline? This includes: OS security settings, EHR security configurations, email security settings, cloud service security configurations, and firewall rules.
guidance: Security settings can change over time due to updates, staff actions, or vendor changes. Without a documented baseline, you cannot verify that configurations are correct. Review security settings at least quarterly for critical systems and annually for all systems. Document any deviations and remediation actions.
lookFor: Documented security settings for each ePHI system
Quarterly review schedule for critical system configurations
Annual review for all systems
Deviations documented with remediation timelines
Evidence of at least one configuration review in the past year

title: Mandatory Encryption at Rest (2026 Rule)
description: Is all ePHI encrypted at rest on all devices and systems?
guidance: Under the 2026 rule, encryption is no longer 'addressable' — it becomes mandatory for all ePHI at rest. This includes workstations, servers, databases, backups, removable media, and cloud storage. AES-256 or equivalent is expected. Full-disk encryption alone may not suffice if databases or file shares contain unencrypted ePHI accessible to compromised accounts.
lookFor: Full-disk encryption on all workstations and laptops (BitLocker, FileVault)
Database-level encryption on all ePHI databases
Cloud storage encryption (at-rest) confirmed for all services
Backup encryption verified
No unencrypted ePHI on removable media

title: Mandatory Encryption in Transit (2026 Rule)
description: Is all ePHI encrypted in transit (email, data transfers, remote access)?
guidance: All ePHI transmitted electronically must use TLS 1.2 or higher encryption. This applies to email, HL7/FHIR interfaces, remote access sessions, cloud API calls, and any data transfer between systems. TLS 1.0 and 1.1 are explicitly prohibited.
lookFor: TLS 1.2+ enforced on all web-facing services
Email encryption for messages containing ePHI
VPN or encrypted tunnels for all remote access
HL7/FHIR interfaces using encrypted channels
TLS 1.0 and 1.1 disabled on all systems

title: Mandatory MFA on All ePHI Systems (2026 Rule)
description: Is multi-factor authentication (MFA) enabled on ALL systems that access ePHI?
guidance: The 2026 rule mandates MFA on every system accessing ePHI — EHR, email, billing, cloud storage, remote access, and administrative consoles. MFA is no longer addressable. App-based authenticators or hardware security keys are preferred; SMS-based MFA is acceptable but discouraged due to SIM-swap risks.
lookFor: MFA enabled on EHR and practice management systems
MFA enabled on email (Microsoft 365, Google Workspace)
MFA enabled on cloud storage and backup portals
MFA required for VPN and remote access
MFA on administrative consoles and privileged accounts

title: Annual Penetration Testing (2026 Rule)
description: Has your practice completed a penetration test within the last 12 months?
guidance: Annual penetration testing becomes a mandatory requirement under the proposed Security Rule update. Testing must be performed by a qualified, independent party and cover both external (internet-facing) and internal (network-level) attack vectors. Results must be documented with a formal remediation plan for identified vulnerabilities.
lookFor: Penetration test report dated within the last 12 months
Testing performed by a qualified, independent third party
Both external and internal testing scopes covered
Formal remediation plan for all findings
Evidence that critical findings were remediated

title: Semi-Annual Vulnerability Scanning (2026 Rule)
description: Has your practice completed vulnerability scanning within the last 6 months?
guidance: Semi-annual vulnerability scanning of all ePHI systems is required. Scans should cover internal network devices, servers, workstations, and external-facing services. Critical and high vulnerabilities must be remediated within defined timelines (typically 14 days for critical, 30 days for high).
lookFor: Vulnerability scan report dated within the last 6 months
All ePHI systems included in scan scope
Critical vulnerabilities remediated within 14 days
High vulnerabilities remediated within 30 days
Scan-to-scan comparison showing remediation progress

title: Comprehensive Audit Logging & Review (2026 Rule)
description: Are audit logs enabled and reviewed on all systems containing ePHI?
guidance: Comprehensive audit logging with regular review becomes mandatory. All systems containing ePHI must log access events (login, logout, data access, modifications, failed attempts) and these logs must be reviewed at a defined frequency. Logs must be protected from tampering and retained for a minimum period.
lookFor: Audit logging enabled on all ePHI systems (EHR, email, cloud, billing)
Logs capture: user ID, timestamp, action, data accessed
Defined log review schedule (weekly or monthly minimum)
Logs protected from modification or deletion
Log retention period documented (6 years for HIPAA)

title: Documented Patch Management Process (2026 Rule)
description: Does your practice have a documented patch management process for all ePHI systems?
guidance: Regular patching of all systems handling ePHI with documented timelines and verification. The 2026 rule expects a formal, written patch management policy that defines: patching cadence by severity, testing procedures, deployment verification, and exception handling for systems that cannot be patched immediately.
lookFor: Written patch management policy document
Defined timelines: critical (14 days), high (30 days), medium (60 days)
Patch testing process before deployment to production
Verification that patches were successfully applied
Exception process for systems that cannot be patched (compensating controls)
